summary: A review of Iris Murdoch's collected letters, revealing her intense dedication to correspondence, her dismissal of certain philosophical and literary peers, and her complex, adventurous romantic life, which she considered her true area of expertise.

principles.statement: Love is the primary subject in which I hold true expertise.
principles.rationale: Despite projecting an aura of 'cold virginity' in public, she cultivated an unusually wide carnal experience and viewed love as central to her understanding of life.
principles.source_quote: Like Socrates, love is the only subject on which I am really expert.

heuristics: Dutifully reply to every letter received, unless the sender is "mad or spiteful."

signature_quotes.context: Describing her joyousness and dependence on writing correspondence to her oldest friend.

signature_quotes.text: Like Socrates, love is the only subject on which I am really expert.
signature_quotes.context: Asserting her deep knowledge of romantic and sexual relationships.

signature_quotes.text: I am a sadomasochistic male homosexual.
signature_quotes.context: Proclaimed by her 'Mr. Hyde' persona, highlighting the paradoxical and complex nature of her sexual identity.

signature_quotes.text: I can't be very interested in her thoughts.
signature_quotes.context: Her critique of Virginia Woolf, acknowledging Woolf's narrative skill but dismissing her intellectual depth.

anti_patterns.description: Logical positivism, as advanced by philosophers like Alfred Ayer.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: She viewed it as lifeless and devoid of philosophical nourishment, calling it "a rattle of dry bones."
